steps.prompt: Which word is the main clause verb?

hints.defendere: 'defendere' is an infinitive meaning 'to defend' — it complements 'audeat'
hints.audeat: 'audeat' is subjunctive in the relative clause, not the main verb

hints.to go: 'to go' is 'ire' — 'erit' comes from 'esse' (to be), future tense
hints.to wander: 'to wander' is 'errare' — 'erit' is the future of 'esse'

hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'erit' is future indicative

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.erro, errare, erravi, erratum: These are the parts of 'errare' (to wander/err)
hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)

hints.Present indicative: Present indicative would be 'audet' — 'audeat' with -eat ending is subjunctive
hints.Imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'auderet' — 'audeat' is present subjunctive

steps.prompt: Why is the subjunctive used with 'audeat'?
steps.target: Relative clause of characteristic — 'the sort of person who would dare'
choices: Relative clause of characteristic — 'the sort of person who would dare'

hints.Purpose clause: Purpose uses 'ut/ne' — this is a relative clause (introduced by 'qui') with subjunctive of characteristic
hints.Result clause: Result uses 'ut' + subjunctive — the 'qui' here introduces a relative clause of characteristic
hints.Indirect question: Indirect questions use interrogative words — 'qui' here is a relative pronoun describing a type of person

hints.audio, audire, audivi, auditum: These are the parts of 'audire' (to hear) — not 'audere' (to dare)
hints.augeo, augere, auxi, auctum: These are the parts of 'augere' (to increase)
hints.aufero, auferre, abstuli, ablatum: These are the parts of 'auferre' (to carry away)

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'tu' — 'te' is accusative, object of 'defendere'
hints.Ablative: 'te' can be ablative, but here it is the direct object of 'defendere'

translation: As long as there will be anyone who dares to defend you.
